ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002694 represents cooked rice as a category; no basmati-specific cooked rice term exists. Used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked rice broadly; no basmati-specific cooked rice facet applied. Used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226355009 represents cooked rice generically; no basmati-specific code identified.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of steamed white basmati rice is approximately 186 g (about 1 cup cooked), as commonly served as a side dish in an Indian restaurant meal.

nutrient_source.title: Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked, unenriched, without salt (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 168878)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Basmati rice cooked values are nutritionally very close to long-grain white rice cooked. USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168878 (cooked, unenriched, without salt) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values are per 100 g cooked weight. Basmati-specific values from ICMR-NIN (Indian Food Composition Tables 2017, item 'Rice, raw, milled, cooked') are consistent with these figures and corroborate the USDA data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; basmati rice, white, boiled (GI = 57)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 57 for white basmati rice (boiled/steamed) is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. GL per serving calculated as (57 × 28.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 186 g serving) / 100 = approximately 30.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (1 mg per 100 g) when cooked without added salt
hypertension.guidance: Steamed white basmati rice cooked without salt is very low in sodium (approximately 1 mg per 100 g) and is suitable for hypertensive individuals as a staple carbohydrate, provided accompaniments such as curries and chutneys are monitored for sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 57) and high carbohydrate density (28.2 g per 100 g cooked)
type2_diabetes.guidance: White basmati rice has a moderate GI of 57 and a glycemic load of approximately 30 per standard serving (186 g), which can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 100–150 g cooked and pair with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1 g per 100 g) and negligible cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed white basmati rice is very low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, making it a heart-neutral staple; however, its low dietary fibre content (0.4 g per 100 g) means it does not actively contribute to LDL reduction, and substituting with brown basmati rice would provide greater cardiovascular benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed white basmati rice (Safed Sada Chawal) is a plain, long-grain white rice dish prepared by washing and steaming or boiling basmati rice in water until fully cooked and fluffy. Basmati rice is prized for its distinctive aromatic fragrance, slender elongated grains, and light, non-sticky texture when cooked correctly. It is a staple carbohydrate in Indian, Pakistani, and broader South Asian cuisine, commonly served as an accompaniment to curries, dals, gravies, and grilled meats. In Singapore, it is widely available at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls, often paired with dishes such as butter chicken, dal makhani, or tandoori items. Nutritionally, per 100 g cooked, it provides approximately 130 kcal, 2.7 g protein, 0.3 g fat, and 28.2 g carbohydrate, with very low sodium (1 mg) and negligible saturated fat. Its glycemic index is 57 (medium), meaning it raises blood glucose more moderately than regular long-grain white rice (GI ~72) but still requires portion control for individuals with type 2 diabetes. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. The dish contains no significant allergens in its plain form.
common_user_queries: How many calories in steamed basmati rice?
Is basmati rice good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168878, cooked long-grain white rice) and corroborated by ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for cooked milled rice. GI of 57 for white basmati rice is directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Both sources are approved primary references under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; white rice contains no trans fats by nature. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; starch is the dominant carbohydrate and free sugars are negligible. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based grain food. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero in cooked white rice; heat and milling remove any residual ascorbic acid. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — naturally zero in white milled rice. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant food. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero in rice. vitamin_e_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked unenriched white rice. Serving size of 186 g (1 cup cooked) is based on USDA standard reference portion for cooked rice. GL calculation: (57 × 28.2 × 186) / 10000 = 30.1. The food_group field in the input JSON was listed as 'Mixed'; corrected to 'Grains and staples' as this is a plain rice dish. Cuisine is Indian per input; multilingual naming requirements applied for Singaporean-context Indian dish (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g values of 0.1 g each are per USDA SR Legacy data for cooked long-grain white rice.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_group from 'Mixed' (input) to 'Grains and staples' (accurate classification).
Added canonical food_name 'Steamed White Basmati Rice' replacing input's 'Safed Sada Chawal' as English canonical name.
Added full multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi.
Populated all per_100g nutrient fields from USDA SR Legacy FDC 168878 and ICMR-NIN.
Added GI=57 (medium) from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008.
Computed GL per serving = 30.1 based on 186 g serving.
Added three health_context conditions with clinical guidance.
Added comprehensive aliases and aliases_by_language with consistency verification.
Documented all zero values in notes with appropriate justification phrases.
Added ontology anchors for FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT with honest closest_parent match types.
